age_group_options.description: Nurturing care for babies focusing on bonding, safety, sensory play, and early milestones.

age_group_options.label: Toddlers (2–3 years)
age_group_options.description: Active exploration, language development, and early social skills for busy toddlers.

age_group_options.label: Preschool (3–5 years)

articles.title: Potty Training Readiness at Daycare
articles.summary: Learn how we assess potty training readiness and partner with families during this milestone.
articles.body:
Potty training is a big step for toddlers and families. At our centers, we look for signs of readiness such as staying dry for longer periods, showing interest in the bathroom, and being able to follow simple directions. We never rush or pressure children; instead, we follow their cues and respect each family’s cultural and developmental expectations.

In the classroom, teachers use positive language, visual cues, and consistent routines to make potty training feel safe and predictable. We encourage families to start introducing the idea at home before we begin active potty training at school. Communication is key: families and teachers stay in touch daily about successes and challenges so we can keep the experience consistent.

We typically begin offering structured potty training support during the toddler and young preschool years, once both the family and the teaching team agree that a child is ready. If you’re unsure whether your child is ready, talk to your classroom teacher or center director for a personalized plan.

articles.title: How We Support Potty Training in Our Curriculum
articles.summary: Discover how potty training fits into our toddler and preschool classroom routines.
articles.body:
Potty training support is woven into our daily toddler and preschool routines. Teachers offer regular bathroom breaks, gentle reminders, and plenty of time so children don’t feel rushed. We use child-sized toilets or adapters, step stools, and visual schedules so children can independently navigate the process.

Our curriculum focuses on self-help skills, body awareness, and building confidence. Books, songs, and role play help normalize bathroom routines and reduce anxiety. We partner closely with families to decide on language ("potty" vs. "bathroom"), timing, and strategies such as using training underwear or pull-ups.

To maintain dignity and privacy, teachers offer help as needed but encourage children to do more independently over time. We never shame or punish accidents; instead, we treat them as a normal part of learning. Families can expect potty training support to begin once children are developmentally ready, typically between ages 2 and 3½.

articles.title: FAQ: Potty Training Policies for Toddlers and Preschoolers
articles.summary: Answers to common questions about when potty training support begins, supplies to bring, and accident policies.
articles.body:
This FAQ covers our most common potty training questions.

**When do you start potty training support during the day?**
We typically begin active potty training support once a child shows signs of readiness and the family requests support. This is most common between ages 2 and 4. Support is offered during regular classroom hours, with scheduled potty times and additional reminders based on each child’s needs.

**What should families bring?**
We ask families to provide several extra changes of clothing, extra socks, and training underwear or pull-ups based on the agreed plan. Please label all items with your child’s name.

**How do you handle accidents?**
Accidents are treated calmly and respectfully. Teachers quietly help children change and clean up, avoiding any shame or punishment. We communicate with families at pick-up about how the day went.

**Do children need to be fully trained to move to the next classroom?**
Potty training is never the only factor in classroom placement. We consider age, developmental readiness, and classroom openings. Our goal is to support each child’s progress at their own pace.

articles.title: Preschool Curriculum Overview (Ages 3–5)
articles.summary: An overview of our play-based preschool curriculum for ages 3–5.
articles.body:
Our preschool curriculum for ages 3–5 is built around play-based, hands-on learning. Children explore early literacy, math, science, and social studies through centers, projects, and outdoor play. Teachers plan experiences that foster curiosity, problem-solving, and cooperation.

Key components include circle time, small-group instruction, choice time, and outdoor learning. Social-emotional skills—such as sharing, resolving conflicts, and expressing feelings—are integrated into every part of the day.

We align our curriculum with widely recognized early learning standards to help prepare children for kindergarten while preserving the joy of childhood. Families receive regular updates about classroom themes and individual progress.

articles.title: A Sample Day in Our Toddler Classrooms
articles.summary: See how meals, naps, potty training, and play fit into a typical toddler day.
articles.body:
A typical toddler day balances structure and flexibility. Children arrive to warm greetings and free play, followed by morning meeting where teachers introduce simple songs, stories, and activities. Snack and outdoor play are built into the morning to support physical development.

The midday hours include lunch, rest time, and quiet activities. Afternoons feature small-group exploration, sensory play, and music and movement. Diaper changes and potty breaks are scheduled at regular intervals, with additional opportunities based on individual needs.

Routines are predictable but flexible enough to respond to children’s cues. Teachers communicate with families through daily reports that summarize meals, naps, potty/diapering, and highlights from the day.

articles.title: How Enrollment Works: Step-by-Step Guide
articles.summary: Learn how to inquire, tour, apply, and complete enrollment at our centers.
articles.body:
Enrollment begins with exploring our programs online and submitting an inquiry or tour request. After your visit, you can complete an application and indicate your preferred start date, campus, and schedule. Our team will confirm availability or offer a spot on the waitlist.

Once a space is offered, families receive enrollment forms, policy acknowledgments, and tuition details. A deposit or registration fee may be required to secure your child’s spot. We then schedule a welcome call and classroom visit so your child can get comfortable before their first day.

Throughout the process, our enrollment team is available to answer questions about tuition, schedules, and program options.

articles.title: Understanding Tuition and Fees
articles.summary: An overview of how tuition is calculated and what is included in your monthly rate.
articles.body:
Tuition is based on your child’s age group, schedule (full-time, part-time, or extended hours), and campus. Our rates include classroom materials, most activities, and daily meals and snacks where offered. Occasional additional fees may apply for special events or optional enrichment programs.

We offer a tuition calculator on our website to help you estimate monthly costs based on your chosen schedule. Families can also discuss financial options, such as sibling discounts or employer-sponsored benefits, with our administrative team.

Tuition is billed monthly, and payments are typically due at the beginning of each month. Detailed tuition and fee policies are outlined in our family handbook.

articles.title: Tours and Visits FAQ
articles.summary: Find answers about scheduling in-person tours, virtual visits, and what to expect.
articles.body:
Families are encouraged to schedule a tour before enrolling. We offer weekday in-person tours at set times, as well as occasional virtual information sessions. During a tour, you’ll see classrooms, outdoor spaces, and common areas, and have time to ask questions.

To schedule, use the "Visit Us" page on our website to choose your preferred campus, date, and time. You’ll receive a confirmation email with visit details, parking information, and check-in instructions.

Children are welcome to attend tours, and we recommend allowing at least 30 minutes for your visit. If you need to reschedule, please contact the center directly or use the reschedule link in your confirmation email.

articles.title: Health and Safety Practices
articles.summary: Learn about our illness policies, cleaning routines, and safety procedures.
articles.body:
The health and safety of children and staff are our top priorities. Classrooms are cleaned and sanitized daily, with high-touch surfaces disinfected throughout the day. Handwashing routines are built into transitions such as arrival, meals, bathroom use, and outdoor play.

We follow state licensing regulations and pediatric health guidelines related to vaccination requirements, exclusion of sick children, and safe food practices. Emergency drills are conducted regularly so staff and children know what to do in case of fire, severe weather, or other emergencies.

Families receive clear communication when there are classroom exposures to contagious illnesses, and our illness policy outlines when children may return to care.

articles.title: Meals, Snacks, and Nutrition
articles.summary: See how we plan child-friendly menus and handle allergies and special diets.
articles.body:
Our menus are designed to offer balanced meals with fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and proteins appropriate for young children. Weekly menus are shared with families so you can see what your child will be served.

We take allergies and dietary restrictions seriously. Families should provide written documentation of allergies, and in some cases an emergency action plan from a healthcare provider. Classroom teachers receive training on safe food handling and allergy protocols.

Infants follow individualized feeding plans that align with family preferences and pediatric guidance. For toddlers and preschoolers, mealtimes also focus on building independence, social skills, and positive attitudes toward new foods.

articles.title: Family Events and Community Connections
articles.summary: Learn about our weekend family events, celebrations, and how to RSVP.
articles.body:
We host a variety of family events throughout the year, including weekend playdates, curriculum nights, and seasonal celebrations. These events are designed to help families connect with teachers, other parents, and our broader community.

Most events are free or low-cost, and many are scheduled on Saturday or Sunday afternoons to accommodate working families. You can view upcoming events and submit RSVPs through the Events & Calendar page on our website.

Event descriptions include age recommendations, activities, and whether siblings are welcome. We encourage families to register early so we can plan materials and staffing appropriately.

articles.title: Our Teachers: Qualifications and Training
articles.summary: Understand the experience, education, and training our teachers bring to the classroom.
articles.body:
Our teachers are experienced early childhood professionals who meet or exceed state licensing requirements. Many hold degrees or credentials in early childhood education or related fields, and all participate in ongoing professional development.

Training topics include child development, positive guidance, health and safety, and inclusive practices. Toddler and preschool teachers receive additional training in curriculum planning, potty training support, and kindergarten readiness.

We carefully screen and onboard new team members with reference checks, background checks, and mentoring from experienced staff.

articles.title: Helping Your Child with Separation Anxiety
articles.summary: Practical tips to make drop-off smoother for both children and caregivers.
articles.body:
It’s normal for young children to experience separation anxiety, especially during transitions like starting daycare or moving to a new classroom. Consistent routines, a brief goodbye ritual, and reassuring language can make a big difference.

We encourage families to talk about school positively, read books about saying goodbye, and allow plenty of time for drop-off so neither adult nor child feels rushed. Teachers offer comfort, engage children in activities, and send updates once children settle.

If separation anxiety persists, we’ll partner with you to develop a tailored plan that supports your child’s emotional needs.

articles.title: Nap Time Routines and Rest Policies
articles.summary: Learn how we handle naps, rest, and quiet time for different age groups.
articles.body:
Rest is essential for growing children. Infant nap schedules are based on individual needs and family preferences, while toddlers and preschoolers have a daily rest period after lunch. Children who do not sleep may engage in quiet activities such as looking at books or drawing.

Families are welcome to provide a small comfort item such as a blanket or stuffed animal, following our safety guidelines. Teachers create a calm environment with soft lighting and soothing music to help children relax.

Our rest policy outlines maximum nap lengths, supervision requirements, and how we support children who have difficulty settling.

articles.title: Illness Policy FAQ
articles.summary: Find out when to keep your child home and when they can return after an illness.
articles.body:
To protect the health of all children and staff, we follow clear guidelines for when children must stay home. Reasons include fever above the threshold specified in our handbook, vomiting, diarrhea, or other symptoms that limit a child’s ability to participate.

Children may return to care once they have been symptom-free for the required time, or after receiving clearance from a healthcare provider when necessary. We communicate updates about classroom illnesses while maintaining family privacy.

Please refer to our full policy document for details on specific conditions. If you’re ever unsure, call your center to discuss your child’s symptoms before arriving.

articles.title: How We Communicate with Families
articles.summary: Daily updates, newsletters, and conferences keep you connected to your child’s day.
articles.body:
Strong family communication is at the heart of our program. Families receive daily updates through our family app or written reports, including notes on meals, naps, potty/diapering, and activities.

We also send email newsletters with center updates, special events, and reminders. Formal conferences are offered at least once per year, and teachers are available to connect at drop-off, pick-up, or by appointment.

Families can update contact preferences at any time and are encouraged to reach out whenever questions or concerns arise.

campuses.name: Downtown Campus
campuses.description: Our Downtown Campus is steps from the Embarcadero and ideal for families who live or work in the Financial District and South Beach. Bright, modern classrooms and an indoor gross-motor space make this an excellent option for infants through preschoolers.

campuses.name: Lakeside Campus
campuses.description: Located near Lake Merced, the Lakeside Campus offers expansive outdoor play areas and nature-inspired learning environments. It serves toddlers, preschoolers, and school-age children with both half-day and full-day options.

campuses.name: Riverside Campus
campuses.description: The Riverside Campus sits along Mission Creek, convenient to SoMa and Mission Bay. Families appreciate its cozy infant rooms, dedicated toddler spaces, and a rooftop play deck with bay views.

campuses.name: Uptown Campus
campuses.description: Our Uptown Campus in Oakland serves a diverse community of families with flexible schedules for infants through school-age children, including before- and after-school programs.

campuses.name: Suburban Campus
campuses.description: The Suburban Campus offers a quieter neighborhood setting with large classrooms, dedicated nap spaces, and easy parking for drop-off and pick-up.

events.title: Spring Family Picnic at Downtown Campus
events.description: Join us for an outdoor spring picnic with bubbles, sidewalk chalk, and light refreshments. Siblings and extended family are welcome.

events.title: Lakeside Family Art & Play Day
events.description: An afternoon of open-ended art stations, music, and sensory play in the Lakeside Campus garden.

events.title: Riverside Music & Mingle Afternoon
events.description: Sing-alongs, movement games, and a mini instrument-making station for the whole family.

events.title: Uptown Family STEM & Science Day
events.description: Hands-on science stations including water play, ramps and balls, and simple experiments. Small materials fee per family.

events.title: Earth Day Celebration at Suburban Campus
events.description: Garden planting, nature crafts, and a neighborhood clean-up walk to celebrate Earth Day.

events.title: Downtown Campus Open House
events.description: Tour classrooms, meet teachers, and learn about our programs and enrollment process.

events.title: Parent Workshop: Potty Training Basics
events.description: Our Lakeside teaching team shares signs of readiness, classroom routines, and how to partner on potty training at home and school.

events.title: Riverside Weekday Morning Playdate
events.description: Drop in with your child for a casual morning of play and a chance to meet other families.

events.title: Family Storytime & Songs
events.description: A cozy Saturday morning of stories, songs, and fingerplays for infants, toddlers, and preschoolers.

events.title: Summer Kickoff Family Fair
events.description: Celebrate the start of summer with outdoor games, art, and a popsicle stand. Small admission fee per family.

events.title: Memorial Day Center Closure
events.description: All campuses will be closed in observance of Memorial Day. We look forward to seeing families the following day.

events.title: Uptown New Family Orientation
events.description: An evening orientation for newly enrolled families to review policies, curriculum, and daily routines.

teachers.bio: Maya has spent the last eight years teaching toddlers in urban childcare centers. She specializes in language-rich classrooms, early social skills, and supporting families through milestones like potty training.
Andre brings a calm, playful energy to the toddler classroom. He loves creating outdoor obstacle courses and supporting emerging independence.
certifications: Associate Degree in Early Childhood Education

teachers.bio: Priya works with older toddlers and young preschoolers at Lakeside, focusing on gentle routines, potty training readiness, and early literacy.
Li has a background in infant development and is known for her soothing presence and strong communication with new parents.
Sofia leads a pre-K classroom at Lakeside, integrating project-based learning, nature walks, and kindergarten readiness skills.
Jordan is the Center Director at the Downtown Campus and has over a decade of experience supporting families and teaching teams.
Chris supports both toddler and preschool classrooms at Uptown and enjoys leading music circles and outdoor games.
Emily teaches young toddlers at the Suburban Campus and focuses on building routines that support independence and language.
Alex works with older preschoolers and school-age children, leading STEM clubs and pre-K readiness groups.
Rachel coordinates the school-age program at Uptown, including homework club and enrichment workshops.
